SUMMARY:
Abstract Microalgae cultivation in photobioreactors (PBRs) has emerged as a promising and sustainable approach to address various environmental and energy challenges, offering a multitude of benefits across diverse applications. Recent developments in microalgae cultivation in photobioreactors have contributed substantially to the development and optimization of sustainable bioprocesses. This review presents a comprehensive analysis of recent innovations and breakthroughs in the field of microalgae cultivation, with a specific focus on their application in photobioreactors, aimed at paving the way for a greener future. This study in-depth examines the advantages of microalgae cultivation in photobioreactors, concentrating on its effectiveness in wastewater treatment, CO 2 bioremediation, and the production of biofuels and high-value products.

==============================================================================================

Highlights
----------

*   •Photobioreactor design and operation depend on physical and biological characteristics. 
*   •Overview of photobioreactor types and scaling up from lab to industrial production characteristics. 
*   •Optimize performance, control, and many operating parameters utilizing AI model. 
*   •Scaling up and industrial production have been eased by photobioreactor design developments. 
*   •Finding the research gap and future perspectives for mass scale microalgae cultivation are recommended. 

Abstract
--------

[Microalgae cultivation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/microalgae-cultivation "Learn more about Microalgae cultivation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in photobioreactors (PBRs) has emerged as a promising and sustainable approach to address various environmental and energy challenges, offering a multitude of benefits across diverse applications. Recent developments in microalgae cultivation in photobioreactors have contributed substantially to the development and optimization of sustainable [bioprocesses](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/bioprocesses "Learn more about bioprocesses from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This review presents a comprehensive analysis of recent innovations and breakthroughs in the field of microalgae cultivation, with a specific focus on their application in photobioreactors, aimed at paving the way for a greener future. This study in-depth examines the advantages of microalgae cultivation in photobioreactors, concentrating on its effectiveness in [wastewater treatment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/wastewater-treatment "Learn more about wastewater treatment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), CO 2 bioremediation, and the production of biofuels and high-value products. The review evaluates the effects of light, [solar irradiation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/solar-irradiation "Learn more about solar irradiation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), temperature, nitrogen and [phosphorus concentrations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/phosphorus-concentration "Learn more about phosphorus concentrations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in culture media, CO 2 concentrations, and pH on microalgae growth performance, including specific growth and [biomass productivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/biomass-productivity "Learn more about biomass productivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The study also examines open systems like unstirred ponds, [raceway](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/raceways "Learn more about raceway from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ponds, and circular ponds and closed systems like horizontal tubular, vertical bubble-column, airlift, flat panel, and plastic-bag photobioreactors, comparing their pros and cons. To optimize microalgae cultivation, key factors in photobioreactor design, including [photosynthetic efficiencies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/photosynthetic-efficiency "Learn more about photosynthetic efficiencies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), light/dark (L/D) cycles, CO 2 concentrations, mass transfer, [hydrodynamics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/hydrodynamics "Learn more about hydrodynamics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") behavior, and pH, are extensively investigated. In addition, the review outlines recent developments in large-scale photobioreactors and highlights the challenges and opportunities associated with photobioreactor scale-up and design parameter optimization, including genetic engineering and [economic feasibility](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/economic-feasibility "Learn more about economic feasibility from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This article is a vital resource for researchers, engineers, and industry professionals seeking sustainable bioprocesses and the application of microalgae-based technologies.

1. Introduction
---------------

[Fossil fuels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/fossil-fuel "Learn more about Fossil fuels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have become a crucial part of modern life, powering energy and electricity production and improving living standards. However, their combustion releases [carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/carbon-dioxide "Learn more about carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CO 2) into the atmosphere, causing global [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib1)]. Saudi Arabia is a significant CO 2 emitter, with industries, electricity, transportation, and households being the major contributors [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib2)]. Energy alone is responsible for over 90% of Saudi Arabia's national CO 2 emissions from fossil fuels [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib3)]. Globally, [power generation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/power-generation "Learn more about power generation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from fossil fuels accounts for one-third of all CO 2 emissions [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib3)]. Therefore, scientists worldwide are working to mitigate CO 2 emissions and combat global warming [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib4)].

As cities grow and industries expand, the amount of wastewater they generate is increasing rapidly. Treating this wastewater is essential for protecting the environment, as excessive nutrients like nitrogen and phosphorus can lead to eutrophication and harm ecosystems [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib5)]. Removing these nutrients from tertiary wastewater is crucial to reduce oxygen requirements in [water bodies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/body-water "Learn more about water bodies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), protect aquatic life, and maintain human health. There are several methods available for nutrient removal, including filtration, membrane technology, precipitation, advanced [oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/alpha-oxidation "Learn more about oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [biological nutrient removal](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/biological-nutrient-removal "Learn more about biological nutrient removal from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). However, these methods can be costly and produce [hazardous waste](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/hazardous-waste "Learn more about hazardous waste from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") sludge [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib6)]. One significant challenge is removing nitrogen and phosphorus simultaneously from tertiary wastewater [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib7)].

[Microalgae](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/microalga "Learn more about Microalgae from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are unicellular organisms that can convert solar energy into [chemical energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/chemical-energy "Learn more about chemical energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") through [photosynthesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/photosystem "Learn more about photosynthesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), using CO 2 as a carbon source [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib8)]. They are more efficient at converting light into energy than other plants. Microalgae can be grown in a photoautotrophic mode, where light is the [primary energy source](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/primary-energy-source "Learn more about primary energy source from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and CO 2 is the carbon source [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib9),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib10)]. They are capable of producing lipids, nitrogen, and phosphorus from media and converting them into valuable food additives and [bioactive compounds](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/bioactive-compound "Learn more about bioactive compounds from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib11),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib12)]. Using wastewater as a growth medium can provide additional benefits, such as removing nitrates, phosphates, and [heavy metals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/heavy-metal "Learn more about heavy metals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib13)]. It is used in the food industry, pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and even cancer treatment [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib14)]. Microalgae are used in various industries, including food, pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and bio-refinery [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib15),[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib16)].

In a photosynthesis process, [microalgae cultivation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/microalgae-cultivation "Learn more about microalgae cultivation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with wastewater offers many advantages to lessen the aforesaid problems, including the following: i) the economic value of the tertiary wastewater effluent in terms of water and nutrient recovery, ii) nitrogen and phosphorus are removed from wastewater simultaneously, iii) microalgae can grow much faster, about 10–50 times higher than terrestrial plant resulting in a CO 2 conversion efficiency into organic compound, iv) generation of large volumes of water that are suitable for recycling on-site and off-site or safe to discharge to [surface water](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/surface-water "Learn more about surface water from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") bodies, v) production of algae biomass that is suitable for [bioenergy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/bioenergy "Learn more about bioenergy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") generation [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib12),[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib17),[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib18)]. A photobioreactor is the place where these [algal species](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/algal-specie "Learn more about algal species from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can be cultivated and harvested with control to achieve the desired output. Mass transfer, the impact of light, and pH are the three major parameters that allow controlling eventually desired output. Scaling up the laboratory scale to industrial is very much necessary to open the door to the microalgal economy. Much effort is necessary to know the behaviors and upgrade to an industrial scale.

Advancements in microalgae engineering signify progress, innovations, and developments in the application of engineering principles and techniques for the manipulation and utilization of microalgae [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib19)]. In simpler terms, it involves the pursuit of more efficient methods to harness the capabilities of these minuscule algae across diverse applications. These strides encompass a spectrum of crucial enhancements. Scientists and researchers are dedicated to breeding microalgae strains characterized by accelerated growth rates, heightened production of valuable compounds such as lipids and proteins, and resilience in the face of varying environmental conditions [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib20)]. Simultaneously, they are refining the design and functionality of cultivation equipment and systems to create optimal growth environments for microalgae, thereby ensuring efficient nutrient utilization and [biomass production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/biomass-production "Learn more about biomass production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib21)]. This optimization extends to the entire microalgae production process, from cultivation to harvesting and [downstream processing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/downstream-processing "Learn more about downstream processing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), with the aim of maximizing yields while minimizing resource consumption [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib22)]. Genetic engineering techniques are being employed to manipulate the genetic makeup of microalgae, enhancing their capacity to produce biofuels or engage in bioremediation, contributing to sustainable environmental practices [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib23)]. Additionally, researchers are developing strategies for recycling and repurposing resources like CO 2 and wastewater within the microalgae cultivation process, a sustainable practice that reduces resource expenditure and economic costs [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib24)].

Most of the parameters align with suitable photobioreactor cultivation and its characteristics. Some parameters such as light irradiance, construction material, and temperature, can be directly related to the design and mode of the photobioreactor. This review discusses different types of photobioreactors currently used for microalgae (open & closed systems), features, advantages, and disadvantages of control systems. Design components, construction and materials, growth parameters, and controls of photobioreactors are key parameters that affect microalgae cultivation. [Hydrodynamics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/hydrodynamics "Learn more about Hydrodynamics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and mass transfer studies for photobioreactors are also discussed with other key parameters. Finally, a comparison is made to discuss photobioreactors according to the most influencing parameters in operation, issues, and challenges that need to be explored in the future. Laboratory-based analysis is highly important to understand to grow suitable [microalgae species](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/microalgae-specie "Learn more about microalgae species from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in normal to harsh environments. A significant challenge for the outdoor mass cultivation that supports the microalgal economy is the transition from laboratory to industrial scale. To understand the behaviors and upgrade to an industrial scale, much effort is required.

2. Advantages of microalgae cultivation in photobioreactors
-----------------------------------------------------------

Microalgae are highly efficient in [photosynthesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/photosystem "Learn more about photosynthesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and have a much faster growth rate than land-based plants. Some species of [microalgae](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/microalga "Learn more about microalgae from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can even be harvested twice a day [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib25)], and their annual yield can be several times than that of traditional food crops [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib26)]. Microalgae can capture more CO 2 from industrial [flue gases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/flue-gas "Learn more about flue gases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") than conventional crops, making them attractive for reducing greenhouse gas emissions [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib27),[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib28)]. Microalgae can be harvested for [biodiesel](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/biodiesel "Learn more about biodiesel from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), food, animal feed, [biogas](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/biogas "Learn more about biogas from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and fertilizer, providing additional revenue and attracting investment [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib20),[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib29)]. Microalgae cultivation in photobioreactors offers numerous advantages that make it a promising and sustainable approach for various applications. The following are some of the main advantages of microalgae cultivations.

### 2.1. Nutrient recycling and wastewater treatment

There is an incredible requirement to expand the most consistent knowledge for the treatment of household wastewater in many developing countries. This type of treatment method ought to achieve numerous conditions, like easy design, utilization of non-modern equipment, good removal efficiency, and minimum capital expenses. Moreover, the regular growth of the population, the rapid rise in urbanization, the expense, and ease of use of land are pivotal factors when selecting a [wastewater treatment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/wastewater-treatment "Learn more about wastewater treatment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") system [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952823000584#bib30)]. Appropriate [wastewater treatment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/wastewater-treatment "Learn more about wastewater treatment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") techniques are generally categorized into three major types—physical, chemical, and biological. These processes might be applied independently or in the arrangement based on the scope and nature of contamination. Phosphorus, nitrogen, and carbon are nutrients that are essential to aquatic life. However, excess of these nutrients in the [water body](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/body-water "Learn more about water body from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") may cause eutrophication and toxicity.

In water treatment process industries, secondary effluent primarily consists of nitrogen and phosphate, which require extensive [tertiary treatment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/tertiary-treatment "Learn more about tertiary treatment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This treatment units are mainly biological treatment types, where nitrate and phosphate are mostly treated. [Phytoremediation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/phytoremediation "Learn more about Phytoremediation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") indicates the bioremediation or elimination of wastes from the environment utilizing microalgae, and it enjoys many returns over other conventional remediation practices that are expensive, absorb enormous energy, and make a substantial quantity of waste. ### Abstract

Sustainable approaches to circular economy in animal agriculture are still poorly developed. Here, we report an approach to reduce gaseous emissions of CO2 and NH3 from animal housing while simultaneously using them to produce value-added biomass. To this end, a cone-shaped, helical photobioreactor was developed that can be integrated into animal housing by being freely suspended, thereby combining a small footprint with a physically robust design. The photobioreactor was coupled with the exhaust air of a chicken house to allow continuous cultivation of a mixed culture of _Arthrospira spec._ (_Spirulina_). Continuous quantification of CO2 and NH3 concentration showed that the coupled algae reactor effectively purifies the exhaust air from the chicken house while producing algal biomass. Typical production rates of greater than 0.3 g/l\*day dry mass were obtained, and continuous operation was possible for several weeks. Morphological, biochemical, and genomic characterization of _Spirulina_ cultures yielded insights into the dynamics and metabolic processes of the microbial community. We anticipate that further optimization of this approach will provide new opportunities for the generation of value-added products from gaseous CO2 and NH3 waste emissions, linking resource-efficient production of microalgae with simultaneous sequestration of animal emissions.

### Key points

_• Coupling a bioreactor with exhaust gases of chicken coop for production of biomass._

_• Spirulina mixed culture removes CO__2_ _and NH__3_ _from chicken house emissions._

_• High growth rates and biodiversity adaptation for nitrogen metabolism._

### Graphical abstract

Towards a sustainable circular economy in livestock farming. The functional coupling of a helical tube photobioreactor with exhaust air from a chicken house enabled the efficient cultivation of Spirulina microalgae while simultaneously sequestering the animals’ CO2 and NH3 emissions.

Introduction
------------

As a result of the growing world population, the global demand for food is increasing, while at the same time, the available arable land is already exhausted and can only be expanded through progressive deforestation. Therefore, the development and provision of performance-increasing and resource-saving, sustainable agricultural production is essential for mankind (Lampridi et al. [2019](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR25 "Lampridi MG, Sørensen CG, Bochtis D (2019) Agricultural sustainability: a review of concepts and methods. Sustainability 11(18):5120. 
https://doi.org/10.3390/su11185120
")). Closed-loop systems that allow highly efficient use of available land and raw materials within a production process would be ideal. Controlled environment agriculture (CEA) in urban areas is one of such approaches that addresses concerns about conventional agriculture and the increasing demand for food (Dsouza et al. [2021](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR12 "Dsouza A, Price GW, Dixon M, Graham T (2021) A conceptual framework for incorporation of composting in closed-loop urban controlled environment agriculture. Sustainability 13(5):2471. 
https://doi.org/10.3390/su13052471
")). Such urban CEA systems specifically include farming techniques like climate-controlled greenhouses and plant factories with artificial lighting that can provide high yields regardless of external environmental conditions. For example, a recent study suggested that vertical farming under optimized conditions could produce up to 600 times of the current global average annual wheat yield (Asseng et al. [2020](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR4 "Asseng S, Guarin JR, Raman M, Monje O, Kiss G, Despommier DD, Meggers FM, Gauthier PPG (2020) Wheat yield potential in controlled-environment vertical farms. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A 117(32):19131. 
https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2002655117
")).

In contrast to the advanced prospects in plant agriculture, sustainable approaches to circular farming in animal agriculture are less well developed. This branch of agriculture, which deals with animals raised for meat, fiber, milk, or other products, imposes a heavy burden on the environment worldwide (Andretta et al. [2021](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR3 "Andretta I, Hickmann FMW, Remus A, Franceschi CH, Mariani AB, Orso C, Kipper M, Létourneau-Montminy M-P, Pomar C (2021) Environmental impacts of pig and poultry production: insights from a systematic review. Frontiers in Veterinary Science 8:750733. 
https://doi.org/10.3389/fvets.2021.750733
"); Gržinić et al. [2023](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR17 "Gržinić G, Piotrowicz-Cieślak A, Klimkowicz-Pawlas A, Górny RL, Ławniczek-Wałczyk A, Piechowicz L, Olkowska E, Potrykus M, Tankiewicz M, Krupka M, Siebielec G, Wolska L (2023) Intensive poultry farming: a review of the impact on the environment and human health. Sci Total Environ 858:160014. 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2022.160014
")). In Germany alone, for example, pigs and poultry are kept on more than 200,000 farms, resulting not only in barely tolerable environmental pollution from feces and manure, but also in enormous emissions of CO2, NH3, and other volatile compounds. While solid and liquid waste emissions from animal husbandry can be converted into more valuable substances with the help of biogas plants (Abanades et al. [2022](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR1 "Abanades S, Abbaspour H, Ahmadi A, Das B, Ehyaei MA, Esmaeilion F, El Haj AM, Hajilounezhad T, Jamali DH, Hmida A, Ozgoli HA, Safari S, AlShabi M, Bani-Hani EH (2022) A critical review of biogas production and usage with legislations framework across the globe. Int J Environ Sci Technol 19(4):3377–3400. 
https://doi.org/10.1007/s13762-021-03301-6
"); Esteves et al. [2019](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR13 "Esteves EMM, Herrera AMN, Esteves VPP, Morgado CdRV (2019) Life cycle assessment of manure biogas production: a review. J Clean Prod 219:411–423. 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2019.02.091
")), approaches to reduce gaseous emissions from animal houses and at the same time use them for the production of valuable substances are scarce.

The upgrading of CO2 to valuable compounds can be achieved by, e.g., photosynthetic microorganisms (PMO), such as eukaryotic (micro)algae or _Cyanobacteria_, which bind CO2 by photosynthesis and which are also able to filter nitrogen and sulfur compounds or other gaseous or aerosol-bound substances from the air. For their growth, these PMO require light, CO2, water, and inorganic nutrients such as nitrogen and phosphorus sources, as well as trace elements. Under optimal conditions, typically with temperatures in the range between 20 and 40 °C, a sufficient carbon source, and light energy, PMO can double their biomass within 12 h and convert large amounts of gaseous emissions into biogenic substances through photosynthesis. It is therefore not surprising that PMO cultivation is an established technology to produce valuable biomass in a resource-saving way by now (Fernandes et al. [2015](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR14 "Fernandes BD, Mota A, Teixeira JA, Vicente AA (2015) Continuous cultivation of photosynthetic microorganisms: approaches, applications and future trends. Biotechnol Adv 33(6, Part 2):1228–1245. 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biotechadv.2015.03.004
")). In PMO cultivation processes, carbon usually accounts for more than half of the dry weight of the biomass produced. However, the PMO cells are also rich in a wide variety of biogenic and bioactive substances, such as amino acids, proteins, carbohydrates, oligo- and polysaccharides, unsaturated fatty acids, and numerous secondary metabolites, such as vitamins, dyes, or antibiotics (Pierobon et al. [2018](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR37 "Pierobon SC, Cheng X, Graham PJ, Nguyen B, Karakolis EG, Sinton D (2018) Emerging microalgae technology: a review. Sustain Energy Fuels 2(1):13–38. 
https://doi.org/10.1039/C7SE00236J
")). Moreover, nowadays, the choice of PMO is not limited to naturally occurring strains, but with the help of genetic engineering, recombinant species are also accessible, producing certain biogenic constituents in high yields. This makes high-value products for energy, food, or health applications, such as enzymes or even therapeutic antibodies accessible (Ahmad et al. [2020](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR2 "Ahmad N, Mehmood MA, Malik S (2020) Recombinant protein production in microalgae: emerging trends. Protein Pept Lett 27(2):105–110. 
https://doi.org/10.2174/0929866526666191014124855
")).

PMO and other microorganisms are also used for bioremediation to remove pollutants from liquid, solid, and gaseous waste streams (Bala et al. [2022](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR5 "Bala S, Garg D, Thirumalesh BV, Sharma M, Sridhar K, Inbaraj BS, Tripathi M (2022) Recent strategies for bioremediation of emerging pollutants: a review for a green and sustainable environment. Toxics 10(8):484. 
https://doi.org/10.3390/toxics10080484
"); Roy et al. [2022](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR41 "Roy A, Gogoi N, Yasmin F, Farooq M (2022) The use of algae for environmental sustainability: trends and future prospects. Environ Sci Pollut Res 29(27):40373–40383. 
https://doi.org/10.1007/s11356-022-19636-7
"); Touliabah et al. [2022](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR48 "Touliabah HE, El-Sheekh MM, Ismail MM, El-Kassas H (2022) A review of microalgae- and Cyanobacteria-based biodegradation of organic pollutants. Molecules 27(3):1141. 
https://doi.org/10.3390/molecules27031141
")). For example, microorganisms on a solid support material can be used as powerful biofilters for air purification to remove air pollutants in exhaust gases (Hussain et al. [2021](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR19 "Hussain K, Haris M, Qamar H, Hussain T, Ahmad G, Ansari MS, Khan AA (2021) Bioremediation of waste gases and polluted soils. In: Panpatte DG, Jhala YK (eds) Microbial rejuvenation of polluted environment: volume 2. Springer Singapore, Singapore, pp 111–137")), and PMO are established for bioremediation of liquid media, such as to improve nitrogen waste management in recirculating aquaculture systems (Ramli et al. [2020](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR40 "Ramli NM, Verreth JAJ, Yusoff FM, Nurulhuda K, Nagao N, Verdegem MCJ (2020) Integration of algae to improve nitrogenous waste management in recirculating aquaculture systems: a review. Frontiers in bioengineering and biotechnology 8:1004.  
https://doi.org/10.3389/fbioe.2020.01004
")). While the use of PMO for CO2 sequestration (Cheah et al. [2015](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR10 "Cheah WY, Show PL, Chang J-S, Ling TC, Juan JC (2015) Biosequestration of atmospheric CO2 and flue gas-containing CO2 by microalgae. Bioresource Technol 184:190–201. 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2014.11.026
"); Kumar et al. [2011](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR23 "Kumar K, Dasgupta CN, Nayak B, Lindblad P, Das D (2011) Development of suitable photobioreactors for CO2 sequestration addressing global warming using green algae and cyanobacteria. Bioresour Technol 102(8):4945–4953. 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2011.01.054
"); Onyeaka et al. [2021](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR34 "Onyeaka H, Miri T, Obileke K, Hart A, Anumudu C, Al-Sharify ZT (2021) Minimizing carbon footprint via microalgae as a biological capture. Carbon Capture Sci Technol 1:100007. 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ccst.2021.100007
")) is an established approach as it allows for simultaneous air cleaning and biomass production, its application for reducing gas emissions from animal housing is not well studied.

Suitable photobioreactors, which could be used under the conditions given in conventional animal houses to utilize animal emissions (e.g., chemical components contained in the air) and waste heat from the animals for the production of valuable substances with the help of PMO, would have to meet certain requirements. The photobioreactor should be directly and easily integrated into existing animal barns. Since conventional barns require the entire floor space for the animals (pigs, cattle, sheep, poultry, etc.), a suitable device should have the smallest possible footprint and should have high physical robustness to avoid mechanical damage to the reactor and its operating elements (light fixtures, pumps, valves, etc.). In addition, a suitable photobioreactor should be scalable in terms of size and culture volume, and should be easily integrated into serial or parallel arrangements. Last but not least, the reactor should have the best possible performance in terms of PMO culture efficiency.

Of the almost infinite number of closed photobioreactors described in the literature (Kunjapur and Eldridge [2010](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR24 "Kunjapur AM, Eldridge RB (2010) Photobioreactor design for commercial biofuel production from microalgae. Ind Eng Chem Res 49(8):3516–3526. 
https://doi.org/10.1021/ie901459u
")), we chose a cone-shaped, helical tubular reactor (Watanabe and Hall [1996](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR51 "Watanabe Y, Hall DO (1996) Photosynthetic production of the filamentous cyanobacterium Spirulina platensis in a cone-shaped helical tubular photobioreactor. Appl Microbiol Biotechnol 44(6):693–698. 
https://doi.org/10.1007/BF00178604
")), as this allows combining a small footprint with a robust design when the reaction tube is made of transparent flexible tubing material and such a device is implemented in a suspended structure. We report here for the first time the performance of a prototype of such a suspended helical cone photobioreactor for reducing gas emissions from a chicken house to produce _Spirulina_ (_Arthrospira spec._), a cyanobacterium (Fig. [1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#Fig1)). Quantification of CO2 and NH3 concentration showed that the coupled algae reactor effectively purifies the exhaust air from the chicken house while producing algal biomass. Continuous operation was possible for several weeks. Metagenomics analysis of the _Spirulina_ culture showed that exhaust air from the chicken house altered the composition of the algal culture and provided clues to NH3 metabolism.

The exhaust air from the coop is taken by an air pump via a CO2 “input” sensor and blown into the bioreactor via the air inlet at the bottom, rises into the reservoir via the helical tube, and escapes via the gas outlet and the CO2 “output” sensor. The buoyancy of the rising air transports the medium through the helix into the reservoir, and then flows back from there through a central return tube into the lower part of the helix. **B** Technical drawing and **C** photographic image of the helical tube photobioreactor. Materials and methods
---------------------

### Microalgae cultivation

Of the photosynthetic microorganisms (PMO) considered to be of commercial importance, species of the genus _Spirulina_, now called _Arthrospira spec._, are among the most important (Gonçalves et al. [2016](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR15 "Gonçalves AL, Rodrigues CM, Pires JCM, Simões M (2016) The effect of increasing CO2 concentrations on its capture, biomass production and wastewater bioremediation by microalgae and cyanobacteria. Algal Res 14:127–136. 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.algal.2016.01.008
"); Soni et al. [2019](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#ref-CR44 "Soni RA, Sudhakar K, Rana RS (2019) Comparative study on the growth performance of Spirulina platensis on modifying culture media. Energy Rep 5:327–336. 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.egyr.2019.02.009
")). In this study, a mixed culture of _Spirulina_ microalgae (_Arthrospira platensis_), kindly provided from an open pond culture of a microalgae farm (ROVAL GmbH, Rockstedt, Germany), was used as a starter culture for continuous cultivation in closed photobioreactors. The culture medium was prepared from tap water supplemented with 1.45 g/l Agrolution 324 fertilizer (ICL Fertilizers, Ludwigshafen, Germany), 0.19 g/l KALISOP (K + S Aktiengesellschaft, Kassel, Germany), 0.033 g/l Micromax WS Fe Chelate (ICL Fertilizers), 1 g/l Canna Trace Mix Mono (CANNA GmbH, Kleve, Germany), 55 g/l sodium chloride (Südwestdeutsche Salzwerke AG, Heilbronn, Germany), and 9 g/l sodium bicarbonate (Höfer Chemie GmbH, Kleinblittersdorf, Germany) to obtain final concentrations of 203 mg/l N, 51 mg/l P, 344 mg/l K, 35 mg/l S, 4.3 mg/l Fe, and 17 mg/l Mg. To grow inoculum for the helical tube reactor, the starting culture obtained from ROVAL was first propagated in conventional 10 l algabags (Algatec, Sottrum, Germany) under aeration with regular air for about 10 days until the optical density at 565 nm was about 2.0. Thirty liters of this algae suspension together with 115 l of nutrient medium was filled into the helical tube reactor via the media inlet (Fig. [1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#Fig1)) and aeration inside the reactor was started with an air supply of 15 l/min. Cultivation was performed in air-lift mode under continuous illumination with 15 LED lamps, 28 W each (L28 AP673L, VALOYA, Helsinki, Finland), installed inside the helical cone (see Fig. [1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#Fig1)). The progress of algal growth was determined by sampling (see the “[Analytics](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#Sec4)” section), and when the biomass of about 0.9–1 g dry weight per liter was reached (typically in 3–4 days), parts of the biomass were removed by rotational filtration. For this purpose, 90 l of algal suspension was taken through the reactor’s media outlet and filtered using a drum filter (Spirutech, Entrepierres, France). The harvested cleared media was post-fertilized with 720 g/kg (harvested dry weight) Agrolution 324, 65 g/kg KALISOP, 10 g/kg Micromax WS Fe chelate, 100 g/kg Canna Trace Mix Mono, and 2000 g/kg sodium bicarbonate and then returned to the helical tube reactor through the media inlet for the next growth cycle. When the growth rates observed in the cycle dropped (typically after 4–6 weeks), the entire long-term cultivation process was restarted with fresh inoculum as described above.

### Analytics

To determine growth, aliquots of the algal suspension were taken at regular intervals through the reactor outlet and the biomass contained therein was determined photometrically by absorbance measurement at 565 nm (HI801 Iris Photometer, Hanna Instruments, Vöhringen, Germany). To convert the absorbance values to dry mass, a calibration curve was prepared by measuring corresponding samples after vacuum filtration and drying in triplicates (Figure [S1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-023-12815-7#MOESM1)).

The pH of the aqueous solutions was determined using a pH meter (PH22 digital, PCE Deutschland GmbH, Meschede, Germany). For morphological characterization of algae, a light microscope (Ortholux II, Leitz, Wetzlar, Germany) was used. To determine the CO2 concentration in the exhaust air of the chicken house before (input CO2) and after (output CO2) passage through the algae reactor, T6545 meters (COMET SYSTEM, Bezručova, Czech Republic) were used. Data point intervals were every 15 min, and calibration to 400 ppm was performed for both instruments simultaneously on outside air as specified by the manufacturer. For the quantification of NH3, Hydrion Ammonia Test Paper strips (Micro Essential Laboratory Inc., New York City, USA) were moistened with distilled water according to the manufacturer’s instructions and placed in the gas stream for 15 s and read immediately from the color scale on the test kit (see Figure S2). To increase statistical confidence, these measurements were performed repeatedly over a longer period of time (\> 30 days) and the individually obtained values (_n_ \> 10) were averaged.

### Sequencing analysis

For the extraction of genomic DNA (gDNA), 1 ml of algae suspension was collected in a 2 ml sterile Eppendorf tube. The suspension was centrifuged for 20 min at 16,000 × g to remove the excess of medium/water. By using the commercial DNeasy PowerSoil Pro Kit (QIAGEN, Hilden, Germany), the gDNA of the remaining pellet was extracted by following the manufacturer’s instructions. The concentration of extracted gDNA was quantified photometrically by NanoDrop OneC Microvolume and fluorometrically by Qubit 3 (both from Thermo Scientific Inc.). Metagenomics libraries from the purified gDNA were prepared by using the NEBNext® Ultra™ II FS DNA Library Pro Kit for Illumina® (New England Biolabs, Germany), after which the quality of the libraries was verified using the Agilent High Sensitivity DNA Kit on the Agilent 2100 Bioanalyzer instrument (Agilent Technologies, Germany). DNA libraries were sequenced on an Illumina NextSeq550® (New England Biolabs, Germany) device by using 300 cycles and a paired-end approach.

# Photobioreactors

The industrial cultivation of algae relies on photobioreactor (PBR) technology, which depends on robust glass tubing that’s strong, UV-stable, bio-secure, and food-safe. SCHOTT’s PBR solutions provide the highest quality glass tubing with exceptional light transmission for reliable, long-lasting equipment.

## Making the most of nature

Microalgae are regarded as an exciting source of vegan protein and nutrients used in food supplements for a wide range of health benefits. While microalgae are commonly found in nature, industrial cultivation is an efficient method of mass production, with photobioreactors (PBR) producing large amounts of consistent high quality and uncontaminated microalgae. DURAN® borosilicate glass tubing is widely used in PBR technology, providing the ideal environment for biomass production and water purification.

## How to choose a PBR?

Phototrophic algae can generally be cultivated in two different ways, in open or in closed systems. The main difference between these two approaches is whether the algae solution is separated from the atmosphere or not. This has a crucial influence on which environmental impacts can affect the algae solution, and how well the algae cultivation can be controlled.

**Advantages of closed systems at a glance:**

Significantly higher productivity

Lower risk of contamination

Low water consumption

Low space usage

Excellent purity of the biomass (food grade)

High reproducibility of results

Constant and predictable production volume

Cost efficient harvest due to higher algae concentration

The possibility of an easy change of the cultivated algae

Low weather dependency

The possibility of a 24h production with artificial lighting

## Methods

### Open systems

Algae can be cultivated in natural open ponds. However, in industrial production, artificial man-made ponds are usually found. These are typically flat and no deeper than 30 cm. In the water of these ponds, the algae are able to conduct photosynthesis and form biomass with the help of sunlight radiation. Open ponds are typically built in circular or raceway configurations. The open raceway pond is particularly common. The water is kept in motion, for example by paddle wheels, to intermix the algae.

#### The advantages and disadvantages of open systems

Open ponds are seemingly inexpensive and easy to build. However, danger of contamination, high water evaporation and low volumetric productivities are the main challenges, which lead to lower quality biomass output, large water uptake and costly downstream processes. Due to the constant water loss, the ponds often over salt over time. Some difficulties can be overcome by rooftops – however, at a higher cost.

### Closed systems

With algae cultivation in closed systems, the cultivation solution in comparison to open ponds, is found in closed containers. The algae are therefore isolated from the immediate atmosphere. There are different forms of closed systems, they are mainly dominated by tubular and flat panel reactors. Other options are bags, coils or domes. The algae in closed systems, depending on their construction are set into motion by induced CO2 or pumps.

#### The advantages and disadvantages of closed systems

Closed systems initially require a higher investment and more knowledge about algae cultivation and production conditions. A closed system however, rewards one with significantly higher and especially reliable productivity and quality. This is because the impactful factors on cultivation in a closed system can be controlled accurately, one avoids bacterial contamination as well as dirt ingress, and the design permits a more effective use of light compared to open ponds. Additionally, closed systems are able to produce food grade biomass.

### Where should closed systems be applied

Closed cultivation methods come especially into consideration when high quality biomass is the main cultivation goal. Or when the algae need an accurately controlled environment for their development. This is the case when the intended use of the algae is for human nutrition, as an integral part of cosmetics or in pharmaceutical products. Ideally, these products are produced with a cultivation method that is food grade.

### Comparison of open ponds and closed systems

![Table showing comparison of open ponds and closed systems](https://media.schott.com/api/public/content/f719734aee694e1a964e65071783929e?v=af22c629)

![Table showing comparison of open ponds and closed systems](https://media.schott.com/api/public/content/f719734aee694e1a964e65071783929e?v=af22c629)

The geographical location of the algae cultivation and the available space are crucial for the choice of the cultivation method. These two factors potentially eliminate an open PBR. Furthermore, the significantly higher productivity and product quality as well as the enhanced calculability of production are also compelling reasons for using a closed cultivation method. There are numerous possibilities to choose from. The most common method of closed algae cultivation is the production in tubular PBRs, flat panel reactors and plastic bags.

**Advantages of tubular PBRs at a glance:**

Regardless of the container material, due to their geometry, tubular PBRs have the following advantages in comparison to other closed systems:

Higher productivity, due to optimal light usage in the tubes and simultaneous rare downtime.

Excellent calculability of production, due to their hardly every being unplanned production interruption.

These advantages are especially applicable to tubular systems made of glass.Glass systems additionally convince by:

Less biofilm formation, based on the low surface roughness of glass.

Easier cleanability with chemical and mechanical methods.

A very long lifespan of up to 50 years.

The lowest total cost of ownership at long operating periods.

## Types of closed systems

### Tubular PBR systems

Tubes made of glass or polymer are arranged vertically or horizontally. In most cases a pump enables the algae culture to pass through the tubular system, after which the culture is collected by a tank and recycled again into the tubular system. Tubular PBRs allow to plan production reliably, considering the processes, the amount and quality. Moreover, they are especially productive, due to them being able to use the available floor space and available light radiation perfectly. They are also relatively easy to clean.

### Flat panel PBR systems

This system uses plates made of glass or polymer. The plates are arranged vertically or horizontally with a thin layer of algae culture in between. The systems provide good lighting conditions. The systems however, suffer from heating problems and the tendency to form excess biofilm, especially when polymer plates are used. These biofilms are difficult to clean, as they are mechanically hard to access.

### Plastic bag PBR systems

Plastic bags made of PVC or PE are fixed on special holder systems to intercept the culture supernatants. The investment costs are low, but strong biofilm formation followed by a frequent replacement of bags makes the process labour intensive and causes a lot of waste.

### Where should tubular PBRs be applied

Closed tubular photobioreactors exist in laboratory and production sizes, as they are the only systems, which are able to produce high quality biomass for a cost efficient production of high end algae products like DHA, astaxanthin or spirulina. Algae for dietary supplements are best grown in closed systems as they provide a high degree of purity and productivity, which results in high quality of the final product. Furthermore, glass as a container guarantees that one can produce at a food grade level.

The central components of a closed PBR are the containers, in which the algae cultivation takes place. These containers can be made of either transparent plastic or glass. Both materials have advantages and disadvantages that have an influence on how often the production has to be interrupted and how high the PBRs total cost of ownership is. These factors then have an impact on the production costs of the algae.

## Materials, from which PBRs are constructed

### Polyethylene (PE)

Polyethylene as a polymer variety is commonly used during algae cultivation in plastic bags. Bags of PE are particularly cost efficient at procurement. They typically however, need to be replaced at the latest, after one season or a year. This is due to them being easily covered by algae and their poor clean ability. The replacement is linked to material costs and lots of manual labor, and is therefore expensive.

### Polyvinylchloride (PVC)

Polyvinylchloride is typically used for flat panel and tubular reactors. The procurement costs of these systems at a given volume are cheaper than those systems made of glass. Due to PVC not being able to transmit the entire light spectrum, algae cultivation in these systems is less productive than with other materials. Furthermore, PVC degrades very quickly under UV radiation that it needs to replaced, when being used outside, every 2-4 years.

### Polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA)

A plastic alternative for the manufacturing of tubular reactors is the use of polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA or Perspex). Compared to PVC, PMMA has the advantage of transmitting the entire light spectrum and hardly degrading. The lifespan of PMMA under solar lighting is approximately 10 years. However, a PMMA reactor of the same volume costs more than a glass reactor, which lasts 50 years. Additionally, the issue of biofilm formation is common for plastics such as PMMA.

### Conclusion regarding PE, PVC and PMMA

Generally speaking, to ensure that photobioreactors made of plastic can produce at the same degree of efficiency, the plastic elements must be replaced within operation comparably often. When regarding longer operation periods of the reactor, this results in an unfavourable total cost of ownership. Moreover, some polymer varieties emit substances into the algae solution, resulting in algae cultivation at food grade not being possible.

### Borosilicate glass

Tubular PBRs are almost always made of borosilicate glass. This glass offers numerous advantages compared to other polymer varieties. It allows the entire light spectrum to reach the algae inside the tube. It is resistant against UV radiation, chemicals and salt water. This is the reason why tubes made of borosilicate glass are just as productive after 50 years as they are at delivery. They are also not very susceptible to biofilm formation. If this should occur at all, they can be easily cleaned.

**Benefits of borosilicate glass:**

**Light transmission**

Excellent light transmission

No solarisation or browning effect

No UV-protective additive or coating necessary to secure material properties

Lifetime of borosilicate glass > 50 years

**Fire protection**

Glass does not burn or give off toxic fumes

**Leaching**

Glass is a chemically highly resistant material. With plastic, depending on the polymer type, monomers or oligomers of hazardous substances such as bisphenol-molecules can be leached into the algae culture.

**Cleaning**

Mechanical stability allows continuous in-line cleaning with polymer pellets

Chemical stability allows cleaning in place (CIP)

Lower material and maintenance costs compared to quality polymer

**Thermal stability**

Tubes only: No need for expansion loops due to low thermal expansion.

Example: for 5.5 m long tubes and a temperature increase of 20 °C/ 36 °F the expansion of Borosilicate glass is only 0.36 mm/0.01’’ while polymers expand from 3.3-8.8 mm/0.13’’-0.35’’ depending on polymer type

**Cost saving**

Glass tubes can last fifty years and longer

Tubes only: Reduced number of racks due to high mechanical stability, which allows increased tube support distances without sagging of tubes

Example: double distance compared to PMMA tubes

Tubes only: Reduced number of connections due to long tube lengths of 5.5 m

**Sagging (Tubes only)**

No permanent deformation of glass tubes in contrast to polymer tubes

No remaining puddles in tubes when emptying the system

If your main concerns are the algae production costs, the lifetime of the PBR system and the total cost of ownership, you are definitely making the right choice with a tubular glass PBR. However, even when choosing among glass PBRs, there are significant differences. These differences can have a profound impact on the final system performance and therefore it is crucial that you are aware of them.

**Advantages of high end glass at a glance:**

Long lifespan of your photobioreactor of up to 50 years due to mechanically tension free and stable glass

Higher productivity through thinner tube wall thickness

Easy, tension free construction of the PBRs without gaps due to the same tube lengths

Low biofilm build-up

Rare production interruption because of cleaning

Integrated complete system due to coordinated components

## Why high end glass is the best choice for your photobioreactor

### Borosilicate glass – the best glass for a photobioreactor

The glass of the PBR tubes need to withstand numerous environmental impacts and cleaning processes, so that you can productively cultivate algae within them over many years. This is why SCHOTT uses borosilicate glass. This glass type is:

UV-stable: The glass’ light transmittance stays almost constant, even when being exposed to decades of solar radiation and UV radiation.

Chemically stable: Therefore, the glass tubes can be cleaned and disinfected with numerous chemical solutions to remove bio fouling within the reactor.

Salt resistant: This is especially important when  planning on cultivating saltwater algae.

Find out more about the physical and chemical characteristics of borosilicate glass in our factsheet about borosilicate glass. Download the datasheet about borosicate glass.

### Why is high end glass better?

The right type of glass is not the only relevant aspect when choosing glass tubes for a PBR. Just as important is the way the glass is manufactured, how the glass tube is processed and how the system as a whole is designed. Therefore, high end glass components with high productivity and excellent cost benefit calculation differ from lower quality ones.

Highlights
----------

*   •
    
    Palm oil mill effluents (POME) are a nutrient source for microalgae cultivation.
    
*   •
    
    3-stage photobioreactor modeling via fundamental approach.
    
*   •

*   •
    
    Simulation shows energy and resource efficiencies attainable via the system.
    

Abstract
--------

Microalgae have advantages, including rapid growth rates, a high lipid production capacity, effective removal of nitrates and phosphates from wastewater, and efficient carbon dioxide (CO2) absorption. The optimal operating conditions and strategies of microalgae cultivation can vary significantly from one goal to another. An economic approach to exploring various operating strategies is doable via microalgal process modeling and simulation. Therefore, this study aims to develop a simulation model aimed at enhancing algae growth within a photobioreactor (PBR) system designed to reduce CO2 emissions in palm oil mills. This simulation model is constructed to explore the algae growth CO2 capture efficiency and the influence of oxygen (O2) in the water in the PBR. This study achieved a CO2 capture efficiency of up to 60 % which represents the highest capture, and a dissolved O2 of 20 % was achieved due to the effect of the mass transfer coefficient. Algal growth exhibited a high rate, approximately 1057 g/m3, which could serve as a potential pathway for biodiesel or biobutanol production. Additionally, this study underscores the significant role of the mass transfer coefficient in effectively reducing liquid O2 levels to maximize CO2 capture and achieve a high algae yield. Furthermore, the simulation results reveal that a high concentration of O2 in the water promotes photorespiration, which hampers algal growth and reduces CO2 capture efficiency.

1\. Introduction
----------------

The never-ending increase in energy demand has raised a serious concern about fossil fuel depletion and uncontrolled carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions contributing to global climate change [\[1\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib1), [\[2\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib2). To minimize the consumption of fossil fuels and tackle the rise in temperature, which is associated with greenhouse gas (GHG) like CO2 emissions, sustainable and renewable energy sources are required, such as wind, solar, and microalgae, of which the latter has emerged as one of the most promising renewable energy sources, due to the unique traits of microalgae [\[3\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib3). Microalgae are photosynthetic microorganisms that can rapidly adapt to new environments [\[4\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib4). For instance, various industries generate wastewater daily (e.g., palm oil mill effluents (POME)) in large volumes, seriously polluting the water bodies. The component and characterization of wastewater (e.g., POME) play a fundamental part in the cultivation of microalgae and wastewater treatment. Hence, in the decision-making of an appropriate and economical wastewater treatment method, the treated effluent must adhere to the discharge limitations established by regional environmental regulatory bodies.

Microalgae exhibit high potential growth rates, a high lipid production capacity, effective removal of nitrates and phosphate, high CO2 fixation rates, and low land use compared to other energy crops [\[4\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib4). Furthermore, microalgae can thrive under conditions with high levels of CO2 [\[5\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib5). Hence, this characteristic makes microalgae suitable for capturing CO2 in waste flue gases emitted from industrial facilities or power plants. Moreover, microalgae represent a promising feedstock to produce several second- and third-generation biofuels required to meet the demand from the increase in world population and energy consumption [\[6\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib6). Thus, microalgal cultivation from wastewater and biofuel production has been considered economical [\[7\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib7), [\[8\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib8). One of the cultivation systems that has been tested on the microalgae species _Chlorella_ and _Botryococcus_ is the mixotrophic cultivation system, which revealed the assimilation of organic carbons, nutrients, and CO2 simultaneously, performing both respiration and photosynthesis simultaneously [\[9\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib9), [\[10\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib10). _Chlorella sp._ is a flexible microalgae species with a significant adaption ability in most of the wastewater conditions. Thus, _Chlorella sp._ has been reported to perform well in nutrient removal from wastewater. Furthermore, _Chlorella sp._ is one of the microalgae species that can tolerate high concentrations of CO2. Moreover, high content of lipids in _Chlorella sp._ has been obtained by one of the research projects conducted with a cultivation condition of 2 % CO2 and a 0.26-vvm aeration rate [\[11\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib11), [\[12\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib12). Microalgae cultivation in POME offers an alternative to conventional forms of tertiary wastewater treatment and spontaneously utilizes organic compounds in POME to generate microalgae biomass for lipid production [\[13\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib13).

On the other hand, microalgae showed better adaptability to high concentrations of CO2, up to 20 % from flue gas emissions [\[14\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib14), [\[15\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib15). Thus, consistent CO2 gas supply in a bubble column style such as a photobioreactor (PBR) is essential to maintaining microalgae cultures to enhance microalgae growth [\[16\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib16). Some researchers have adopted a process and developed a kinetic model describing the growth of the microalgae species (_Chlorella vulgaris_); using a similar approach, they also modeled two other distinct microalgal species (_Arthrospira platensis_ and _Porphyridium purpureum_) [\[17\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib17). To better estimate and optimize microalgae productivity under different conditions, process modeling is needed to provide valuable information about the performance of microalgae cultivation systems [\[18\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib18). Microalgal cultivation kinetic modeling is essential for designing proficient PBRs and calculating reactor performance [\[19\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib19), [\[20\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib20). Harun et al. [\[17\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib17) found that the biological growth equations in the developed model were sufficient to predict the growth of the three algal species. Nevertheless, these researchers also proposed that _A. platensis_ had a survival mechanism that was activated by the higher light intensities, which is now disregarded by the model and results in poor model accuracy under conditions of fluctuating light intensities. Additionally, De Bhowmick et al. [\[21\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib21) conducted a study on the growth of _Chlorella minutissima_, where lipid and lutein productions have been modeled as a function of wastewater nitrate concentration. Their study indicates that cultivating _C. minutissima_ using the suggested wastewater cocktail is advantageous, with improved neutral lipid accumulation and the potential for commercially environment-friendly application.

The estimated demand for palm oil will reach an enormous 240 metric tonnes by the year 2050 [\[22\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib22). With such a high demand for palm oil in the future, oil palm by-products and wastes will also enormously rise with the increasing potential demand, hence threatening the environment. With oil palm biomass and POME serving as renewable feedstocks, the increasing potential uses of oil palm by-products present a ripe opportunity for microalgae cultivation and sustainable biohydrogen production. The productivity of microalgal biomass is dependent on the effective utilization of substrate (organic or inorganic CO2), sunlight (illumination), nutrients, and many other parameters, such as bioreactor design parameters, mixing regimes, overcoming mass transfer limitations, and hydrodynamic stresses [\[23\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib23). Microalgae are capable of efficiently fixing CO2 and simultaneously producing biomass for multiple applications, which is considered one of the most promising pathways for carbon capture and utilization [\[24\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib24). Some existing findings help advance the understanding of mixing and CO2 mass transfer, important milestones for PBR scale-up studies and partly fulfill the circular economy concept, where microalgae cultivations can address environmental and economic aspects [\[25\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib25). Key factors in the PBR design, including photosynthetic efficiencies, light-dark cycles, CO2 concentrations, mass transfer, hydrodynamic behavior, and pH, have been extensively investigated to optimize microalgae cultivation [\[26\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib26). Several researchers have conducted numerous studies related to the kinetic modeling of microalgae to improve their understanding of the growth and cultivation of algae. However, the majority of the studies were limited to a few parameters and failed to report the dynamics behind the scenes inside the PBR.

Moreover, existing PBR models help to optimize crucial factors such as light, nutrient availability, and CO2 supply to support efficient microalgae growth, and they can predict biomass production, making them useful for designing scalable, cost-effective systems. They often incorporate important biological processes such as photosynthesis dynamics, carbon limitation, and nutrient uptake, offering insights into the physiological responses of algae. However, these PBR models have limitations in accurately accounting for light attenuation due to pigment absorption, scattering, and shading, and they may simplify complex biological interactions, such as photorespiration. Additionally, their focus on ideal conditions makes them less reliable in real-world applications with variables, such as contaminations or fluctuating environments. Despite these limitations, they remain valuable tools for PBR design but require further refinement for broader applications The most relevant features of the PBR model are the inclusion of an allowance for carbon limitation on the growth of microalgae, as well as the dynamic model of photosynthesis and photolimitation and the description of the effect of photorespiration. However, this model was limited to account for the light attenuation caused by pigment absorption and by the scattering and shading effect of the microalgae cells.

The key aspects of the PBR model include accounting for carbon limitation on microalgae growth, along with a dynamic representation of photosynthesis, photosynthesis, and the impact of photorespiration. However, the model is restricted in its ability to factor in light attenuation caused by pigment absorption as well as the scattering and shading effects of microalgae cells. A comprehensive and universal model framework was built for microalgae growth dynamics to guide the scale-up design of PBR to integrate Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), light transfer, and growth rate with aeration and stirring for mixing. Their model not only fills the gap in the microalgae growth dynamic model in the transient stirred PBR but also supports the view that combining the CFD method with the microalgae growth rate is helpful for PBR scale-up design and optimization. However, the model couldn’t clearly explain how to tackle the DO in the PBR which has been the dynamic bottleneck in PBR model design. Gao et al. [\[27\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib27) and Pozzobon and Perre [\[28\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib28) coupled algae growth with CFD in their Fluent and Open-FOAM models, respectively. However, only a few researchers had integrated these modules for consideration, and almost all of the existing models used aeration as the mixing strategy. However, their model couldn’t support the universal transient stirring situation and aeration simultaneously, which is more realistic and applicable, but Jin et al. [\[29\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib29) bridge this gap by conducting a comprehensive and universal model framework built for microalgae growth dynamics to guide the scale-up design of PBR to integrates CFD, light transfer, and growth rate with aeration and stirring for mixing. Their model not only fills the gap in the microalgae growth dynamic model in the transient stirred PBR but also supports the view that combining the CFD method with the microalgae growth rate is helpful for PBR scale-up design and optimization. However, the model couldn’t clearly explain how to tackle the DO in the PBR which has been the dynamic bottleneck in PBR model design. It is also important to know that a well-designed bioreactor requires a comprehensive understanding of factors such as light distribution, mass transfer, shear stress, scalability, and the biology of algal cells. No single bioreactor meets all the requirements of an ideal system. However, hybrid reactors have demonstrated greater effectiveness in large-scale algae production than individual bioreactors [\[30\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib30). Therefore, combining different bioreactor designs to develop more efficient systems for mass algal cultivation should be explored.

Research on enhanced biomass yield can help increase the cultivation efficiency for biodiesel production by _Chlorella sp._ For instance, Bhuyar et al. [\[31\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib31) found out that the nutrient sources present in POME could be potentially used to capture carbon or CO2 and reduce the economic impact of carbon emissions. Recently, Sathinathan et al. [\[32\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#bib32) confirmed that limited studies that incorporate the cultivation of microalgae using industrial wastewater in a PBR with optimizations of parameters using industrial wastewater in the PBR to maximize the microalgae growth are crucial since this remains an open gap yet to be addressed. PBRs are systems that use light to cultivate microorganisms, such as algae, for various purposes, including biofuel production and carbon capture. One of the main challenges in this field is the dynamic nature of the PBR system. The behavior of the PBR system can change over time due to various factors, e.g., excess buildup of dissolved oxygen in PBR, nutrient availability, and cell growth. System dynamics can significantly impact the performance and efficiency of the PBR. Up to date, the PBR dynamic behavior in the bioenergy field, specifically in areas such as H2 production, algae cultivation, and CO2 capture, still presents a significant research problem.

This study aims to bridge this research gap by performing a dynamic simulation to evaluate microalgae capabilities in capturing CO2 using POME as a growth substrate (wastewater) and also to figure out the parameters responsible for the PBR dynamic behavior. The goals of the modeling are to predict the time-dependent substrate/nutrient, algal concentrations, and discharge flue gas compositions (e.g., CO2, and O2) from a PBR system in response to some input changes. Also, the simulation aims to deploy different control strategies to address the dynamic issues that hinder the high yield of algae growth and CO2 capture. These goals lead to the following research questions:

*   a.
    
    How does the dissolved O2 (DO) affect CO2 capture?
    
*   b.
    
    How does the utilization of microalgae cultivated in PBRs with POME affect maximum CO2 capture?
    

These questions represent the bottleneck in understanding the factors affecting the effective operation of a bubble-column PBR. Answering these questions requires this study to explore the PBR system through an extensive simulation. Alternatively, researchers may answer these questions via an experimental approach, but it can be very costly and time-consuming. The present model's development and ability to simulate a closed PBR system based on an existing model modification of an opened microalgae cultivation system represent a significant research contribution. The developed PBR model can help address potential areas for improved efficiency, stability, flexibility, real-time monitoring, and potential for scale-up. The model can effectively be validated using experimental data with remarkable precision and accuracy, which provides valuable insights, predictive capabilities, cost and time efficiency, and a basis for further research. This achievement contributes to the scientific community's tool for understanding and opens up new avenues for exploration and innovation. Furthermore, the simulation based on the model contributes to understanding the complex relationship between CO2 capture and microalgae growth, hence identifying a potential or practical solution to maximizing both objectives. Significantly, it highlights the potential for sustainable and efficient carbon utilization strategies, offering a pathway towards mitigating climate change while promoting beneficial algae growth.

2\. Process description of 3-stage bubble column PBR
----------------------------------------------------

[Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1369703X2400319X#fig0005) shows the schematic of the 3-stage PBR configuration for algal cultivation. In a theoretical setup, there are three PBRs arranged in vertical columns in a single module. Linking multiple PBRs in series creates a nutrient gradient, where fresh nutrients enter the first PBR and are gradually depleted as the culture progresses through subsequent PBRs, allowing different growth stages to occur and optimizing overall productivity. This configuration allows for the accumulation of biomass to reach a desired concentration at the 3rd stage, hence enabling cost-effective microalgae harvesting. Furthermore, this configuration also enhances light utilization since multiple PBRs increase the total surface area to volume ratio, as microalgae experience varying light intensities at the different stages, boosting biomass production overall. Operational flexibility including controllability is improved by facilitating contaminant control, whereby a contaminated PBR can be isolated without disrupting the entire system, and by ensuring process continuity, as other PBRs can continue functioning during disruptions. It supports improved microalgae productivity through strain optimization, as different growth phases can be maintained in separate PBRs, and better population control can be achieved, reducing the risks of overpopulation or underutilization of resources. The scalability of the system is enhanced by allowing modular expansion, where additional PBRs can be added with minimal changes, and by improving resource efficiency, as the effluent from one PBR can serve as an input for the next, reducing waste. The transfer of microalgae between PBRs also enables adaptation to varying conditions, leading to a more robust culture and supporting semi-continuous or continuous production, thus maintaining a steady state of microalgae cultivation.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Photobioreactors harness sunlight to drive photosynthesis, enabling efficient growth of microalgae and enhancing biomass production for various applications.
PBRs are widely used in producing high-value algae products, including biofuels, nutraceuticals, and pharmaceuticals, while also aiding in CO2 sequestration and wastewater treatment.
Photobioreactors provide a renewable energy source by converting sunlight into biomass, contributing to sustainable practices and reducing reliance on fossil fuels.
Effective light penetration is critical; however, in dense algal cultures, shading effects can limit productivity, necessitating design improvements to maximize light exposure.
